headlines.title: Fatal pedestrian crash under investigation in San Jose
headlines.description: Police are investigating a fatal collision involving a pedestrian in a San Jose neighborhood. The crash occurred near Glen Donegal Drive and Glen Ferguson Court, according to authorities. Several surrounding streets have been closed and are expected to remain closed for several hours while investigators work at the scene. Police are asking drivers to avoid the area and use alternate routes. No additional information about the collision was immediately available. The investigation is ongoing.

headlines.title: Man, woman seriously injured in San Jose stabbing
headlines.description: Police are investigating a stabbing that left two people with life-threatening injuries early Friday morning in San Jose. The incident occurred in the 80 block of Descanso Drive, where an adult man and an adult woman were found with stab wounds, police said. Both victims were transported to a local hospital with life-threatening injuries. Police said the motive and circumstances surrounding the stabbing remain under investigation. Authorities added that there is no ongoing threat to public safety. No additional information was immediately available.

headlines.title: 1 dead, 4 injured in fiery I-280 crash in San Francisco
headlines.description: One person died and four others were injured in a fiery crash early Friday morning on Interstate 280 in San Francisco, according to the California Highway Patrol. The crash happened shortly after midnight when a vehicle carrying five people flew off the 18th Street overpass and landed on the southbound lanes of I-280. The vehicle caught fire after the crash. Four people were able to get out, but one person became trapped inside and died, CHP said. The four survivors were taken to a hospital and are recovering. Southbound lanes of I-280 reopened shortly after 7 a.m. The cause of the crash remains under investigation.

headlines.title: Massive fire destroys medical supply warehouse, sparks evacuations in Tracy
headlines.description: A medical supply warehouse was destroyed after it went up in flames and prompted evacuations of neighboring buildings Thursday afternoon in Tracy. The massive blaze, which was one of several fires reported on the 5700 block of Promotory Parkway in the San Joaquin County city, could be seen from across the Bay Area region. Firefighters pointed to water supply challenges and weather conditions as the reason the blaze spread quickly and engulfed the approximately 1 million-square-foot facility. No injuries have been reported, officials said. Officers were also on scene assisting with evacuations and directing traffic, according to police. A police spokesperson said explosions can be heard at the warehouse as flames continued to burn. Police have also told residents impacted by the smoke to close their windows and to shelter in place. Medline Industries warehouse destroyed in Tracy fire NBC Bay Area’s SkyRanger showed the gutted building belonged to Medline Industries. The company has a distribution center located in the area. Medline provided the following statement: “Medline can confirm a fire is occurring at our Tracy, California distribution facility. All Medline employees and on-site personnel are accounted for. The safety and well-being of our employees is our top priority. We are coordinating closely with local authorities and first responders as we assess the fire’s impact and will share more details as they become available.” Fire breaks out at neighboring FedEx warehouse after blaze prompted evacuations Tracy Mayor Dan Arriola said there was no mandatory evacuations in place due to the blaze, but added several nearby businesses in the area self-evacuated. Police said all warehouses southwest of the Medline building have been evacuated: “We are aware of multiple fires in the area and are coordinating with personnel from South San Joaquin County Fire Authority to assess conditions and address any evacuation needs if they arise. Out of an abundance of caution, public safety personnel are present in the Tracy Hills area; however, there is currently no threat to residents. Personnel are assisting with traffic control and monitoring conditions.” The neighboring warehouse evacuated during the blaze is FedEx’s Tracy hub. The company provided the following statement: “We are aware of a building fire that started at a neighboring location before spreading to the Tracy hub. All team members have been safely evacuated and are accounted for. As this is an active situation, further questions should be directed to the local authorities.” At around 7 p.m., authorities said an additional fire broke out at the nearby FedEx warehouse, burning pallets and multiple big rig trailers. Officials said firefighters “performed an aggressive fire attack” to bring it under control. They added that the exterior of building caught fire, but the flames did not engulf the building. Tracy’s city manager declares local state of emergency As around 200 fire personnel combat the fire, authorities said the city’s manager declared a local state of emergency to help ensure that all available resources and emergency response capabilities are available to assist. How did the fire spread so quickly in the large Tracy warehouse? The blaze was reported just before 1 p.m. When firefighters arrived, they found a “rapidly developing fire involving the roof” of the warehouse. Officials said firefighters faced water supply challenges with no water pressure in the building. In addition, sprinklers installed in the warehouse were not working. “Early suppression efforts were hampered by a failure of the building’s private fire water system, which significantly impacted initial firefighting operations,” the City of Tracy said in a statement. “Combined with high winds, low humidity, and elevated temperatures, the fire quickly extended from the roof into the interior of the facility.” A cause of the fire is still under investigation, police said. How could the fire impact air quality in surrounding areas? According to the San Joaquin Valley Air Quality District, as of Thursday afternoon there was no indications the smoke was impacting the air quality, because the smoke is high and up off the ground. However, authorities said as the night falls and temperatures dop, there is concern the smoke could fall as well. Because of this, they advised residents to go inside their homes if they smell smoke, keep their doors and windows shut and run their HVAC. If they don’t have a place to go, officials advised them to go to any place with air conditioning. The warning comes as authorities said they don’t know what’s in the smoke, so they said, “it’s best not to breathe it.” The San Joaquin Valley Air Quality District said they will keep monitoring the air quality in the neighborhood near the fire throughout the night. Warehouse fire sparks mutual aid response In addition to Tracy firefighters and Cal Fire crews, several fire departments from the Bay Area responded to the massive warehouse blaze. The Alameda County Fire Department sent over a strike team and additional resources to the blaze. The Contra Costa County Fire Protection District sent over two engines to help provide coverage in the city. Tracy Fire Department Chief Randall Bradley said it is possible that crews will remain on the area for several days. Napa County Feb 24 Alameda County May 22, 2025 This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.
headlines.source: Kristofer Noceda, John Zuchelli, Jodi Hernandez, Victoria Meza and Alyssa Goard

headlines.title: Thousands pack downtown San Jose for World Cup kickoff
headlines.description: The party is still going strong in downtown San Jose after thousands of soccer fans packed San Pedro Square to watch Mexico defeat South Africa in the opening match of the FIFA World Cup on Thursday. The historic kickoff of the global tournament brought a sea of bright green Mexico national team jerseys to the South Bay, with some fans arriving before 8 a.m.—four hours before the noon match began. The celebration continued throughout the evening as the South Korea vs. Czechia match drew another crowd to San Pedro Square. During the opening match, the crowd was overwhelmingly unified. Virtually no South African jerseys could be spotted in the square as local fans gathered to cheer on El Tri. For many, the tournament opener was reason enough to put their daily routines on hold. “No work today. Today Mexico plays,” said Jesus Villasano, a Mexico fan who skipped work for the event. “Party day. Party day. We waited four years for this.” The festive atmosphere featured a live mariachi band playing traditional songs for the crowd of Mexican and Mexican American fans. The massive turnout also brought a welcome wave of customers to downtown businesses, drawing a visit from San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan. “There’s a lot of people who called in sick today, Mayor,” noted NBC Bay Area reporter Damian Trujillo during the broadcast. “That’s right. It’s World Cup, guys,” Mahan responded. “You get a free pass today.” Mahan said the event represents a major economic boost for local restaurants and bar owners, as well as a significant victory for regional sports fans. More than 20,000 people have RSVP’d for the opening-day watch party at San Pedro Square, according to the San Jose Earthquakes, which is co-hosting the event. Organizers have erected giant 27-by-13-foot television screens in the middle of the square, while local bars and restaurants prepare for packed houses. The local events coincide with the Bay Area’s role as a World Cup host. Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara — temporarily renamed San Francisco Bay Area Stadium for the tournament due to FIFA sponsorship regulations — will host six World Cup matches, starting with Qatar versus Switzerland on Saturday. For fans who do not have tickets to the stadium matches, local organizers are trying to recreate the atmosphere in public spaces. “It’s incredible because you can feel it,” said Gary Peralta of San Jose. “When you know there’s a stadium, you can feel it, but also you’re having watch parties for games, so it’s like you’re there anyway.” “Any time you get people to come down in the vicinity of your business, you have a time to shine,” said Douglas Cookerly, vice president of operations for Sushi Confidential. “You have an opportunity to generate new customers, you give the existing customers interaction, it’s really about all of it together.” The San Pedro Square site will broadcast every match of the tournament. However, city organizers have scheduled various other free, public watch parties across San Jose to highlight the city’s diverse cultural neighborhoods: — Eastridge Center and the Mexican Heritage Plaza will broadcast all Mexico matches. — The Japantown Night Market will feature Japan versus Sweden, as well as the United States versus Turkey match. — Santana Row on Winchester Boulevard will host a viewing for the Colombia versus Congo match later this month. — The Plant on Monterey Highway will broadcast the Round of 32 matches. — Alum Rock Village will host quarterfinal viewings. — Little Saigon will show the playoff rounds and the World Cup final.

headlines.title: After failed 911 calls, man's death may be linked to California's flawed upgrade
headlines.description: When Rickey Spivey Towner had a heart attack in his Coachella Valley home last September, his stepdaughter Megan Conner found him unconscious and called 911. But there was a problem: The equipment used to answer 911 calls at the Desert Hot Springs Police Department malfunctioned and Conner couldn’t connect with a dispatcher for more than two minutes, according to dispatch records obtained by NBC Bay Area. In a recording of one of Conner’s 911 calls, the dispatcher is immediately disconnected, and Conner is met by silence for 25 seconds until the dispatcher can get back on the line. Towner did not survive. His family said he died of a heart attack. Ricky Spivey Towner’s death is the first documented fatality that may be linked to Cal OES’ problematic 911 upgrade. Towner’s death may be the first documented fatality potentially linked to the state’s ongoing 911 system overhaul. Newly obtained records under the California Public Records Act reveal the connection problems were linked to call processing equipment approved by the state as part of California’s troubled Next Generation 911 rollout, sold by a state contractor called NGA 911, and deployed by the Desert Hot Springs Police Department in February 2023. Police records reveal emergency dispatchers were unexpectedly logged out of their phone system as Conner called 911 to report her stepfather lying unresponsive on the floor. Records obtained by NBC Bay Area show all of the dispatchers were logged out of their systems when the 911 call came in. It’s unclear if Towner could have been saved had his stepdaughter been able to summon help faster, but records show a police dispatch manager sent a scathing email shortly after his death to NGA 911. She also copied top officials with the California Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES). “People’s lives are on the line and your failed system may have just cost this person their life,” the dispatch manager wrote on September 12, 2025. “I believe that your engineers are continuously making changes to our live environment which is affecting our user experience. Which again is unacceptable, especially when I had continuously asked you to stop.” Desert Hot Springs dispatch manager’s email to NGA 911 and Cal OES shortly after Towner’s death. Records from the police department lay out the details of what went wrong and show Conner had to call 911 at least three times that morning before she was finally able to relay any information to a dispatcher. It took nearly two-and-a-half minutes. The national standard is for dispatchers to answer 90% of 911 calls within 15 seconds. The equipment that failed is called call processing equipment (CPE) and it was purchased by Desert Hot Springs police after Cal OES suspended new sales of existing call processing equipment and began pushing dispatch centers toward cloud-based systems designed for the state’s Next Generation 911 network. State officials say the Next Generation 911 project is a critical upgrade to California’s antiquated 911 system and will improve emergency response. After a series of reports by NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit, however, the overhaul has faced mounting scrutiny from lawmakers over delays, technical problems and a rising price tag exceeding $450 million. WATCH NBC BAY AREA’S INVESTIGATIONS CA 911: TOO BIG TO FAIL Like most emergency dispatch centers across California, Desert Hot Springs has not switched over to the new Next Gen 911 network. However, it was among the first to use the new cloud-based CPE that Cal OES promoted after suspending sales of legacy call processing equipment that failed to meet Next Generation 911 standards. NGA 911’s equipment had passed state lab testing conducted by Cal OES and was among a handful of vendors approved to sell the new cloud-based CPE when Desert Hot Springs purchased its equipment. While Cal OES, NGA 911, and Desert Hot Springs police were discussing the equipment failure during Conner’s 911 call, the family says they were left in the dark. They say nobody had told them about what happened until they were recently contacted by NBC Bay Area. “Why didn’t I know any of this,” said Lakisha Romero, Towner’s daughter. “My dad has been talked about around the state and I had no clue what was going on.” Lakisha Romero (left) and Megan Conner (right). A timeline of “major events and challenges” in the state’s implementation of Next Gen 911 that has since been posted on Cal OES’ website shows the CPE purchased by Desert Hot Springs had been plagued by persistent problems since it was first deployed more than two years before Towner’s death. “911 calls that were disconnected before being answered by the [911 center] are not displaying for dispatchers,” the Cal OES timeline states. “A workaround was immediately implemented that required dispatchers to use third party technology. NGA 911, LLC is notified of the problem and indicates it is working on a solution.” About a year later, the Wasco Police Department also purchased NGA 911 call processing equipment and experienced “the same problems as [Desert Hot Springs],” according to Cal OES. By May 2025, police in Desert Hot Springs and Wasco had opened roughly 300 trouble tickets concerning issues with NGA 911’s CPE, including 17 of “critical importance” and 99 of “high importance.” Two months later, both departments canceled their CPE orders with NGA 911. In a statement, the Desert Hot Springs Police Department said it “worked collaboratively with NGA and Cal OES regarding operational and technical concerns that arose during implementation and operation.” Cal OES said it took “immediate steps” to help both departments swap out the problematic CPE with equipment from a new vendor, but the process took months to complete and had not occurred before Towner died. Three weeks after his death, Cal OES said it removed NGA 911 from the approved CPE vendor list and the agency eventually cancelled the company’s CPE contract in March of this year. Cal OES declined an on-camera interview request but said in an email the agency is committed to oversight and accountability of its contractors. NGA has not responded to NBC Bay Area’s repeated requests for comment regarding Towner’s death and the equipment failure in Desert Hot Springs. It has posted this timeline on its website explaining its project record in California. California’s Next Generation 911 project is years behind schedule, but state officials say there’s a new plan in place to get the project moving forward again and hope to have the Los Angeles region hooked up to the network in time for the 2028 Olympics. The state agency recently requested another $142 million to meet that goal, which would be paid for by an additional 13 cent surcharge on the phone bill of Californians. As the state moves forward with Next Generation 911 and upgraded call processing equipment that 911 centers desperately need, Towner’s family continues to seek answers. Romero visited the Desert Hot Springs Police Department in May to get some clarity about what happened in her father’s case but said she was disappointed by the response. Lakisha Romero is still searching for answers about what went wrong. “I went asking for answers and nobody wanted to tell me anything,” Romero said. In a statement, the department said it’s “committed to transparency and reliable emergency response services.” Towner’s family said nobody has contacted them about the long history of problems associated with the 911 equipment and questioned why it wasn’t removed a long time ago. “Why should it take someone dying for them to do that,” Romero asked. Candice Nguyen is the reporter on this story. If you have a comment or a question, email her at candice.nguyen@nbcuni.com. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.
headlines.source: Candice Nguyen, Michael Bott and Jeremy Carroll

headlines.title: Some World Cup fans say they are frustrated by FIFA's ticketing and customer service
headlines.description: Several soccer fans who paid thousands of dollars for World Cup tickets say they are frustrated with FIFA’s ticketing process, prompting complaints to NBC Los Angeles and scrutiny from state attorneys general. Long Beach resident Joe Mello said he bought four tickets to the Belgium-Iran match at SoFi Stadium, paying $1,600. But only one ticket initially appeared in his FIFA app, leaving him worried as the Father’s Day game approached. 2026 World Cup Jun 9 “The frustration was not knowing where I was sitting, not knowing if they were going to get it fixed in time and not being able to talk to anyone who seemed to know what they were doing,” Mello said. Mello said FIFA eventually fixed the problem after he spent about a week calling and emailing the organization. He said he believes the situation could have become a “disaster” at the stadium had he not been persistent. Other fans say their complaints center not on missing tickets, but on the seats they received. Jose Flores, a soccer fan from Downey, said he and his wife spent $3,800 on two tickets to Los Angeles’ first World Cup match, the United States against Paraguay. Flores said they purchased Category 2 tickets, which FIFA describes on its website as seats “positioned outside of Category 1 areas” and available in both lower and upper tiers. Flores said he expected midlevel seats. Instead, he said, FIFA assigned them to Section 512 at SoFi Stadium, near the top of the venue. “We knew we were going to get OK seats, but we were not expecting to be seated in the 500s section,” Flores said. Flores said he complained to FIFA but was told his seats would not be reassigned. He said the situation became more frustrating when he later saw Category 2 tickets closer to the field listed on FIFA’s website for less than he paid. “Those same Category 2 tickets you can find in the lower section for $800 cheaper,” Flores said. “And that part is extremely frustrating.” NBC Los Angeles contacted FIFA multiple times for comment but did not receive a response. Derek Howard, a longtime trial attorney who teaches sports law at the University of San Francisco, said ticketholders who believe they were misled could consult a consumer attorney. But he said legal action may not move quickly enough to get fans different seats before match day. Howard said fans may get faster attention by filing complaints with the state attorney general’s office. “Here in California, this falls right within the wheelhouse of the attorney general,” Howard said. California Attorney General Rob Bonta’s office said he recently sent FIFA a letter requesting detailed information about its ticketing practices. In the letter, Bonta said California provides legal protections for consumers and has “strict prohibitions on marketing practices that are likely to mislead them.” Bonta’s office is encouraging ticketholders to file complaints, though a spokesperson declined to say how many complaints have been received. California is not the only state examining FIFA’s ticketing practices. The attorneys general of New York and New Jersey have announced a formal investigation. “Being honest about ticket sales is not complicated,” New Jersey Attorney General Matthew Platkin said in a statement. “But FIFA has turned buying a ticket to the World Cup into a gauntlet of confusion, fake scarcity, and impossibly high prices — all at the expense of consumers.” For now, Howard recommends that fans with ticketing issues keep contacting FIFA and document their efforts. Mello said he had to search extensively before finding a way to reach a person at FIFA. “I did an AI search, and it came up with the FIFA information and the cheat codes on how to actually talk to a human, which came in really handy,” he said. Flores said he and his wife still plan to attend the match, despite their frustration. “We’ll have to go to the game, bite the bullet and then see what happens,” he said.

headlines.title: CaliBunga Waterpark in San Jose will not open this summer, source says
headlines.description: CaliBunga Waterpark in San Jose will not be opening this summer, according to a source. The water park, formerly known as Raging Waters, will temporarily close as the city looks to reimagine and expand the facility. The city will be working with another partner to make plans and improvements to get the water park back open in the future. The park will no longer operate under the CaliBunga name. Lake Cunningham Regional Park will remain open and may have additional activities this summer. San Jose Jul 4, 2024 San Jose May 10, 2024 This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Oakland beloved sports bar reopens just in time for the World Cup
headlines.description: Oakland sports fans packed the sidewalks Thursday as one of the city’s most beloved sports bars made its return. Soccer fans aren’t just cheering for their favorite team — they’re celebrating the return of a beloved Oakland sports bar. Less than an hour after opening its doors, the Oakland Athletic Club hit capacity, with fans lining up down the block to get inside. “We missed them and we want them to stay open and stay open in business as long as they can. For us, it was to show support for the World Cup but also for them,” said Fracisco Alvarado of Oakland. “The spirit of Oakland is the spirit of the tailgate,” Oakland Athletic Club co-owner Angela Tsay said. The bar closed in February, but a new local ownership group made up of Oakland business owners stepped in to bring it back. Tsay, who also owns Oaklandish, explains they want to keep Oakland’s sports tradition alive while building a year-round gathering place for the community. “I think it’s important for people to see that there are still people who are willing to make a small bet on Oakland, who believe in Oakland, we want to continue to invest in Oakland and the more we do that, the more it will attract other people and entrepreneurs and investors,” Tsay said. Opening day coincided with the start of the FIFA World Cup, turning the bar’s comeback into a celebration for both soccer fans and Oakland supporters. “It’s very joyful, people greeting each other, like none of us know each other but we are introducing ourselves and just talking about football,” Alvarado said. The Oakland Athletic Club is also the official bar partner of the Oakland Roots and Soul and the Oakland Ballers. While sports is still the main attraction, owners say they’ve rebuilt the kitchen and revamped the food and drink menu, hoping the food becomes as much of a draw as the action on the screens. “We want it to be somewhere that you come here, even not just on game day but for lunch or gameday with a friend, and that the food is a draw,” Tsay said. Visit Oakland CEO Peter Gamez believes the reopening will help drive more visitors to Oakland for day and overnight stays and sends a positive message about the city’s future. “They believe in the town,” Gamez said. “I think other business owners see hope there, they see opportunity and they see a light at the end of the tunnel that our downtown is revitalizing.”

headlines.title: A closer look at how the World Cup impacts local businesses in the South Bay
headlines.description: The big watch party downtown San Jose, as well as the neighborhood gatherings throughout the South Bay, are geared to help fans gather for the games and also to boost local business. The cheers at the Santana Row World Cup watch party meant Mexico got their goal, and so did the Bay Area Host Committee. The BAHC and San Jose are both organizing neighborhood watch parties to give fans a place to gather and to boost local businesses, now and in the long run. This worked at Santana Row, where a FIFA pop-up store is scoring well with fans. “The business has been great, yeah, we’ve seen a lot of people come in and we’ll be here until the end of July even after the event is over we will be here after that,” store manager Manny Bhagat said. It was equally loud and enthusiastic, and even more crowded, at the San Jose watch party at the Eastridge mall. Dilbreet Bhandal was enjoying the game, splitting time at the watch party, preparing food at his two businesses: Great Kahn’s and a Subway. He says the strategy to boost local businesses is working. “More and more people are starting to come in. As you see, in the morning, there wasn’t that many people and now, it’s so many people here, and so much excitement everywhere,” Bhandal said. “I think when people hear about what we’re doing, whether it’s partnering with our small businesses, or hosting these gatherings, there’s a lot of excitement for people to come together,” San Jose City Councilmember Domingo Candelas said. Diana Zepeda, a World Cup fan, said the plan makes sense, and it encourages her to go out and shop local businesses more. “I just got my eyebrows done downstairs and now I’m grabbing food, so I’m heading back to watch the game. Super excited to be here.” A lot of people were sticking around and shopping; the first step to get people to frequent their local businesses now and after the games are over.

headlines.title: San Bruno man builds system to track planes on his home ceiling
headlines.description: A San Bruno man built a system to track planes on his ceiling as they approach his home. Cameron Paczek, an engineer, built a system he calls “Skylight”. it’s open source, and tracks flights as they approach his San Bruno home. The system shows planes coming and going on the ceiling of his house. Using a small Raspberry Pi Computer, a projector, and a radio antenna, Paczek has created what might be the ultimate conversation starter. NBC Bay Area’s Scott Budman has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: Teens arrested and accused of stealing Pokemón cards in San Francisco
headlines.description: Two teens have been arrested after police say they stole Pokémon cards in San Francisco. It happened on May 28. San Francisco police said the victim agreed to meet a public park to sell his collection of trading cards, but instead of paying, one of the two teens pepper-sprayed him, took his cards and got away in a car driven by another teen. Officers used license plate cameras to track the thief and the driver. The next day, they were both arrested and booked into Juvenile Hall.
